exemplar.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and clear focus on the prescribed title and Boxâs aphorism
Coherent, well-structured arguments supported by four precise examples from mathematics and natural sciences
Effective linkage of examples to the central claim
Demonstrates awareness of alternative perspectives (e.g., internal consistency vs. physical applicability, Newtonian vs. relativistic frameworks)
weaknesses: Evaluation of counter-claims remains brief and largely asserted rather than fully developed
Implications for knowledge production and differing epistemic standards are noted but not explored in sufficient depth
No guiding knowledge question is explicitly formulated to frame the analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The official examinerâs total of 8 requires this single criterion, âUnderstanding Knowledge Questions,â to be awarded 8 out of 10. Although the strand assessment placed the work at the lower end of the 7â8 bandâciting its sustained focus, clear arguments, and strong examplesâits limitations in the depth of critical evaluation and the absence of a guiding knowledge question prevent a full â9â10â performance. An 8 reflects a robust achievement: clear, coherent analysis with effective examples, balanced by only moderately developed evaluation of alternative viewpoints and missing meta-level framing.

annotations.comment: The essay shows awareness of alternative viewpoints by noting that the paradox is âtrue within ZermeloâFraenkel set theory but false in our physical world,â demonstrating critical perspective.
The student could enrich this analysis by discussing how such theoretical models drive further research despite lacking empirical support, reinforcing the idea of usefulness.
There is no evaluation of methodological limitations such as falsifiability or empirical testing; adding this would deepen critical engagement with scientific models.
The claim that Euclidean geometry is ânot wrongâ conflates model idealization with absolute truth; the student should distinguish between model validity and domain of applicability.
The citation style for the Euclid source is informal; the student should use a consistent academic format and, if possible, cite a scholarly secondary source for Euclidean geometry.
The explanation of Euclidean geometry is clear and accurately describes its axiomatic basis and real-world limitations, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
The introduction clearly states the prescribed title and identifies both mathematics and natural sciences as areas of knowledge, establishing a coherent scope for the discussion.
The thesis lacks an explicit knowledge question to frame the essay; the student should formulate a clear KQ to guide analysis and focus evaluation throughout.
The definition of âmodelâ remains vague and descriptive; the student should refine it with ToK terminology and distinguish between model, theory, and framework.
The introductory paragraph repeats the claim rather than critically assessing its extent; a more nuanced stance on how âwrongâ and âusefulâ apply in each AOK would strengthen focus.

The link to Boxâs criteria of simplicity versus accuracy is implied but not explicit; the student should directly discuss how Euclidâs simplicity contributes to usefulness despite known inaccuracies.
The BanachâTarski paradox is described clearly, but the essay lacks deeper philosophical evaluation of its implications for the nature of mathematical truth and physical reality.
The counterclaim regarding non-measurability is valid but underdeveloped; the student should evaluate its significance by contrasting internal consistency with empirical applicability.
There is no transitional link explaining why analysis shifts from mathematics to natural sciences; a bridging sentence that reaffirms the overarching knowledge question would improve coherence.
The concluding sentence generalizes usefulness without directly tying back to Boxâs aphorism; a clearer statement on how Newtonian mechanics exemplifies âsome models are usefulâ is needed.
The Alcubierre drive example is well-chosen to illustrate a mathematically rigorous model that is impractical, directly aligning with the claim about utility.
The discussion notes astronomical energy requirements but provides no quantitative figures; including approximate values would strengthen the argumentâs credibility.
Einsteinâs quote is evocative but is not analyzed in context; the student should explicitly link it back to Boxâs aphorism and the essayâs main arguments.
Throughout the essay, a formal knowledge question has been missing; the student must introduce and revisit a KQ to structure the evaluation and maintain focus.
The citation of Newtonâs Principia via an encyclopedia entry weakens historical context; the student should reference Newtonâs original publication or reputable historical analyses.
The analysis of Newtonian mechanics effectively highlights its simplifying assumptions and predictive successes, illustrating how idealized models can be useful.
The student asserts iterative scientific progress but does not explore how empirical anomalies prompted the shift to relativity; adding this would deepen the evaluation.
This dense paragraph would benefit from subdivision into discrete points (e.g., assumptions, applications, limitations) to enhance logical flow and reader clarity.
The conclusion summarizes examples but lacks explicit synthesis addressing âto what extentâ the claim holds; the student should evaluate the overall balance between wrongness and usefulness.

The final statement that the âquest for the perfect models may be attainableâ contradicts the central thesis; it should be revised to reflect the persistent tension between idealization and practicality.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
